professor.name: Dr. Okafor
professor.text: We've been discussing how markets set prices for popular events this semester. When concert or sports tickets sell out within minutes, resellers often relist them for many times their face value. Some economists argue that price caps on resale protect ordinary fans from being priced out, while others insist that caps simply push transactions onto unregulated black markets and end up harming the very fans they aim to help. Should governments cap ticket resale prices? Why or why not?

students.name: Claire
students.text: I think a cap is justified. Live events carry genuine cultural value, and letting a handful of resellers hoard inventory to extract enormous markups effectively excludes anyone without disposable income. A reasonable ceiling preserves fairer access without abolishing legitimate resale entirely.

students.name: Paul
students.text: Caps sound appealing but rarely function as intended. When the legal price is held artificially low, transactions migrate to informal channels where buyers have no protection against fraud. I'd rather see organizers adopt identity-linked tickets, which confront scalping at its actual source.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: A growing number of school districts have quietly dropped cursive handwriting from the curriculum to make room for typing and coding. Supporters of the change say instructional time is scarce and keyboards dominate modern life, while others worry that abandoning cursive erodes fine motor skills and cuts students off from reading historical documents in their original form. Should elementary schools still require students to learn cursive? Defend your position.

students.name: Mia
students.text: Honestly, requiring cursive feels like nostalgia dressed up as pedagogy. The hours spent perfecting loops could go toward typing fluency or introductory programming, skills students will genuinely depend on later. Historical documents are almost always available in transcribed, searchable versions anyway.

students.name: Carlos
students.text: I'd retain it. Forming letters by hand strengthens memory and motor coordination in ways typing simply doesn't replicate, and there's lasting value in reading a handwritten letter or archival record firsthand. Dropping it trades a durable capability for short-term convenience.

professor.name: Dr. Novak
professor.text: We've been examining how everyday equipment contributes to urban pollution this semester. A single gas-powered leaf blower can emit more hydrocarbons in one hour than a car driven several hundred miles, and its noise carries for blocks. Some residents want cities to ban them outright in favor of electric models, while landscaping crews counter that the electric alternatives still lack the power and battery life for professional work. Should cities ban gas-powered leaf blowers?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: A ban makes sense to me. These machines concentrate disproportionate emissions and noise in dense neighborhoods, affecting residents who never consented to the exposure. Battery technology has improved enough that most residential jobs can now be handled cleanly, and professionals will adapt as they always have.

students.name: Ryan
students.text: I sympathize with the health concerns, but an outright ban overlooks practical constraints. Commercial crews clearing large properties need sustained power that current batteries can't reliably deliver across a full workday. Phasing in incentives for electric equipment would achieve the same objective without crippling small businesses.

professor.name: Dr. Hassan
professor.text: Retailers now present staggering variety, offering dozens of nearly identical products for even the most trivial purchase. Psychologists have recently revived a long-running debate about whether this abundance actually benefits us. Some researchers maintain that more options let people match a choice precisely to their preferences, while others find that excessive choice breeds anxiety, regret, and outright paralysis. Does having more choices generally make people more satisfied, or less? Explain your view.

students.name: Lena
students.text: I lean toward the view that abundance quietly undermines satisfaction. Faced with too many options, people tend to fixate on the alternatives they rejected and second-guess the decision endlessly. A curated set of a few solid choices usually leaves us more confident and content.

students.name: Jonah
students.text: I see it differently. Choice overload is real, but the remedy isn't fewer options, it's better tools for filtering them. Abundance is fundamentally empowering because it allows individuals with unusual needs to find something that genuinely fits, rather than settling for a bland compromise.
